   165	constrains only that the value be one of the listed strings.
   166	
   167	### 3.3 Payload shape
   168	
   169	The `payload` object's shape depends on `kind`.
   170	
   171	- `"plan"` — MUST contain `experiment_id` (string). MAY contain
   172	  planner-specific hints.
   173	- `"implement"` — MUST contain `proposal_id` (string) referring to
   174	  a proposal with `state == "ready"` at the time of dispatch.
   175	- `"evaluate"` — MUST contain `trial_id` (string) referring to a
   176	  trial with `status == "starting"` and a `commit_sha` set.
   177	
   178	### 3.4 Claim object
   179	
   180	| Field | Required | Type | Description |
   181	|---|---|---|---|
   182	| `token` | yes | string | Opaque unforgeable claim token. |
   183	| `worker_id` | yes | string | Opaque identifier for the claiming worker. |
   184	| `claimed_at` | yes | timestamp | When the claim was issued. |
   185	| `expires_at` | no | timestamp | When the task store MAY reclaim. |
   186	
   187	The `claim` field MUST be present when `state ∈ {"claimed",
   188	"submitted"}` and MUST NOT be present for any other state. The
   189	claim is retained through `"submitted"` so that the task protocol
   190	can authorize resubmission by token
   191	([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.2); it is cleared
   192	on reclamation and on the terminal transition to `"completed"` or
   193	`"failed"`. The schema in
   194	[`schemas/task.schema.json`](schemas/task.schema.json) enforces this
   195	presence rule.
   196	
   197	## 4. Event
   198	
   199	Schema: [`schemas/event.schema.json`](schemas/event.schema.json).
   200	
   201	An event records a state change. Events are immutable once appended.
   202	
   203	### 4.1 Envelope
   204	
   205	Every event MUST provide this envelope:
   206	
   207	| Field | Required | Type | Description |
   208	|---|---|---|---|
   209	| `event_id` | yes | string | Unique identifier within the log. |
   210	| `type` | yes | string | A dotted type name, e.g. `task.claimed`, `trial.evaluated`. |
   211	| `occurred_at` | yes | timestamp | When the state change happened. |
   212	| `experiment_id` | yes | string | The experiment this event belongs to. |
   213	| `data` | yes | object | Type-specific payload (§4.2). |
   214	
   215	### 4.2 Type-specific payload
   216	
   217	Each event `type` corresponds to a shape under `data`. The full
   218	enumeration of types is defined in
   219	[`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md); at this layer, the
   220	data model requires only that `data` be a JSON object and that its
   221	shape match the registered schema for the event's `type`.
   222	
   223	### 4.3 Ordering
   224	
   225	Events within a single experiment MUST form a total order consistent
   226	with causality: if event B describes a state change that depended
   227	on a state recorded by event A, A MUST precede B in the log. The
   228	ordering mechanism is a storage concern
   229	([`08-storage.md`](08-storage.md)).
   230	
   231	## 5. Proposal
   232	
   233	Schema: [`schemas/proposal.schema.json`](schemas/proposal.schema.json).
   234	
   235	A proposal is the planner's output.
   236	
   237	### 5.1 Fields
   238	
   239	| Field | Required | Type | Description |
   240	|---|---|---|---|
   241	| `proposal_id` | yes | string | Unique identifier. |
   242	| `experiment_id` | yes | string | The experiment this proposal belongs to. |
   243	| `slug` | yes | string | Human-readable short label. MUST match `^[a-z0-9][a-z0-9-]*$`. |
   244	| `priority` | yes | number | Ordering hint; higher dispatches earlier. |
   245	| `parent_commits` | yes | array of SHA | One or more commit SHAs the proposal is based on. |
   246	| `artifacts_uri` | yes | string (URI) | Where the planner's output documents live. |
   247	| `state` | yes | string | One of `"drafting"`, `"ready"`, `"dispatched"`, `"completed"`. `"completed"` is terminal and means the implementer's attempt has finished (successfully or not); the trial's `status` records the outcome (see [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §7). |
   248	| `created_at` | yes | timestamp | When the proposal was created. |
   249	
   250	### 5.2 Parent commits
   251	
   252	`parent_commits` MUST contain at least one SHA. Each SHA MUST name a
   253	commit reachable from the experiment's starting commit on `main` or
   254	from an already-completed trial on the canonical trial lineage.
   255	
   256	## 6. Metrics schema
   257	
   258	Schema: [`schemas/metrics-schema.schema.json`](schemas/metrics-schema.schema.json).
   259	
   260	A metrics schema is a mapping from metric name to storage type. It

1	# Role Contracts
     2	
     3	This chapter defines the four role contracts that a conforming EDEN
     4	deployment implements: **planner**, **implementer**, **evaluator**,
     5	and **integrator**. For each, it pins what the role observes, what
     6	it produces, and which invariants its output MUST honor.
     7	
     8	A role contract does **not** specify how the role is hosted. A
     9	conforming planner MAY run as a local subprocess, a long-lived
    10	service reached over HTTP, an in-process adapter, or a human at a
    11	keyboard. The protocol constrains the *observable effects* a role
    12	has on the task store, the event log, and the artifact store; how a
    13	role is invoked is a deployment concern and is deferred to a
    14	later-phase *role-binding* specification. Within v0, implementations
    15	MAY document their own binding by defining additional top-level
    16	fields on the experiment config per
    17	[`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §2.4.
    18	
    19	The behavioral state machine that all worker roles participate in —
    20	claim, execute, submit, reclaim — is defined in
    21	[`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md). This chapter describes
    22	only the *role-specific* part of each worker's job: what it reads,
    23	what it produces, and what guarantees its output must carry. Where a
    24	rule depends on a state-machine transition, this chapter cites the
    25	transition by name and points to Chapter 04 for its semantics.
    26	
    27	## 1. Common worker-role lifecycle
    28	
    29	The planner, implementer, and evaluator are **worker roles**: they
    30	participate in the task protocol as consumers. Each worker role
    31	progresses through the same outer lifecycle for every task it
    32	handles:
    33	
    34	1. **Discover.** The worker becomes aware of a pending task whose
    35	   `kind` matches its role. Mechanisms include polling the task
    36	   store, subscribing to an event stream, or receiving a dispatch
    37	   call — all are permitted; none are mandated.
    38	2. **Claim.** The worker atomically moves the task from `pending`
    39	   to `claimed` and receives a claim token. Semantics are in
    40	   [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §3.
    41	3. **Execute.** The worker performs its role-specific work using the
    42	   task payload and any context the protocol grants it (§2–§4
    43	   below). The worker MAY report progress. It MUST NOT mutate any
    44	   protocol-owned object other than (a) the task it holds a claim
    45	   on and (b) the outputs its role is specified to produce (§2.2,
    46	   §3.2, §4.2). The restrictions in §1.2 apply to every worker role
    47	   in addition to the per-role rules below.
    48	4. **Submit.** The worker atomically moves the task from `claimed`
    49	   to `submitted`, presenting its claim token and a result (§2–§4
    50	   below). A submitted task then advances to `completed` or `failed`
    51	   per the rules in [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.
    52	5. **Release on reclaim.** If the task store reclaims the task
    53	   before submit (task store policy per
    54	   [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §5), the worker's
    55	   token is invalidated. A worker that discovers its token has been
    56	   invalidated MUST NOT subsequently attempt to submit against that
    57	   task; it MUST discard any partial result.
    58	
    59	Integrator behavior does **not** follow this worker-role lifecycle.
    60	The integrator is event-driven and is defined separately in §5 below
    61	and in [`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md).
    62	
    63	### 1.1 What a worker MAY assume about its inputs
    64	
    65	Before a task is dispatched to a worker-role queue, the orchestrator
    66	MUST ensure the task's payload references (proposal, trial,
    67	parent commits) satisfy the dispatch preconditions listed per role
    68	below. A worker MAY therefore rely on those preconditions and is not
    69	required to re-validate them. A worker that nevertheless discovers a
    70	violation (e.g. a referenced proposal has been retracted) MUST fail
    71	the task with an error status; it MUST NOT repair store state
    72	itself.
    73	
    74	### 1.2 What a worker MUST NOT do
    75	
    76	- A worker MUST NOT write to any protocol-owned object other than
    77	  the task it holds a claim on and the outputs that task's role is
    78	  defined to produce (§2–§4).
    79	- A worker MUST NOT write to the canonical trial lineage (`trial/*`);
    80	  that is the integrator's exclusive authority
    81	  ([`01-concepts.md`](01-concepts.md) §9,
    82	  [`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md)).
    83	- A worker MUST NOT advance a task's state beyond `submitted`.
    84	  Transitions to `completed` or `failed` are the orchestrator's
    85	  responsibility ([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4).
    86	
    87	## 2. Planner
    88	
    89	The planner proposes what to try. Every planner invocation consumes
    90	one `plan` task and produces zero or more proposals.
    91	
    92	### 2.1 Inputs
    93	
    94	A planner receives:
    95	
    96	- The task object (`kind == "plan"`,
    97	  `payload.experiment_id == E`).
    98	- Read access to the experiment config for `E`
    99	  ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §2), including the
   100	  `metrics_schema` and `objective`.
   101	- Read access to the set of proposals and trials already persisted
   102	  for `E`. The mechanism (direct store read, orchestrator-supplied
   103	  snapshot, event-log replay) is a role-binding concern.
   104	
   105	A planner MAY additionally use any context the experiment config
   106	surfaces to it via role-binding-specific fields. The protocol does
   107	not require the planner to read beyond what is listed above.
   108	
   109	### 2.2 Outputs
   110	
   111	For each proposal the planner produces, it MUST:
   112	
   113	1. Persist a proposal object (via the protocol's proposal store —
   114	   mechanism is binding-specific) that validates against
   115	   [`schemas/proposal.schema.json`](schemas/proposal.schema.json),
   116	   with `experiment_id == E` and `state == "drafting"`.
   117	2. Upload any artifacts (plan text, rationale, supporting files) to
   118	   the artifact store and populate the proposal's `artifacts_uri`.
   119	3. Transition the proposal's `state` to `"ready"` once the proposal
   120	   is dispatchable. The transition from `drafting` to `ready`
   121	   signals that the proposal's metadata is stable and that the
   122	   implementer MAY consume it.
   123	
   124	A planner MAY create multiple proposals under a single `plan` task.
   125	A planner MAY also produce zero proposals if it has no viable
   126	change to suggest; the task still completes normally (see §2.4).
   127	
   128	### 2.3 Proposal invariants
   129	
   130	The planner MUST honor the structural invariants of proposals
   131	([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §5):
   132	
   133	- `parent_commits` MUST contain at least one SHA that names a
   134	  commit reachable from either the experiment's starting commit on
   135	  `main` or a completed trial on the canonical lineage
   136	  ([`01-concepts.md`](01-concepts.md) §9).
   137	- `slug` MUST match the documented pattern.
   138	- `priority` is the planner's own ordering hint; higher values
   139	  SHOULD dispatch earlier.
   140	
   141	### 2.4 Submission
   142	
   143	When its work on a `plan` task is complete, the planner MUST submit
   144	the task (§1 step 4). The submission payload MUST include:
   145	
   146	- `proposal_ids` — the set of identifiers of every proposal the
   147	  planner created under this task. Order is not significant;
   148	  dispatch ordering is determined by each proposal's `priority`
   149	  field ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §5.1). MAY be
   150	  empty.
   151	- `status` — one of `"success"` (the planner completed normally,
   152	  regardless of proposal count) or `"error"` (the planner could
   153	  not complete; any partially-written proposals MUST remain in
   154	  `"drafting"` state and MUST NOT be dispatched).
   155	
   156	A planner MUST NOT submit a `plan` task while any of its
   157	referenced proposals is still in `"drafting"` state.
   158	
   159	## 3. Implementer
   160	
   161	The implementer realizes a proposal as a working-tree change on a
   162	per-trial branch.
   163	
   164	### 3.1 Inputs
   165	
   166	An implementer receives:
   167	
   168	- The task object (`kind == "implement"`,
   169	  `payload.proposal_id == P`).
   170	- Read access to the proposal `P`. The orchestrator MUST ensure
   171	  `P.state == "ready"` before dispatch.
   172	- Read and write access to the git repository associated with
   173	  `P.experiment_id`, at a revision reachable from
   174	  `P.parent_commits`. How the repository is located and how
   175	  access is granted is a role-binding concern (§6,
   176	  [`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §2.4); the protocol
   177	  constrains only that the implementer's writes land under
   178	  `work/*` and that no commit is introduced whose history does
   179	  not descend from `P.parent_commits` (§3.3).
   180	- Read access to the artifacts at `P.artifacts_uri`.
   181	
   182	### 3.2 Outputs
   183	
   184	The implementer produces a **trial** object
   185	([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §7). It MUST:
   186	
   187	1. Persist a trial with `status == "starting"`,
   188	   `experiment_id == P.experiment_id`, `proposal_id == P`, and
   189	   `parent_commits == P.parent_commits`, before making any
   190	   repository write observable to other roles.
   191	2. Write the implementation on a branch under `work/*`
   192	   ([`01-concepts.md`](01-concepts.md) §9) whose parent(s) are the
   193	   proposal's `parent_commits`. Set the trial's `branch` field to
   194	   this branch name.
   195	3. On successful completion, set the trial's `commit_sha` to the
   196	   tip of the `work/*` branch. Optionally set `artifacts_uri` and
   197	   `description`.
   198	
   199	The implementer MUST NOT write to `trial/*` or `main`; those are
   200	owned by the integrator. The `trial_commit_sha` field on the trial
   201	MUST NOT be set by the implementer.
   202	
   203	### 3.3 Worker-branch invariants
   204	
   205	- The `work/*` branch MUST be unique to this trial. Two trials
   206	  MUST NOT share a worker branch name.
   207	- Every commit on the worker branch MUST be reachable from the
   208	  declared `parent_commits`. An implementer MUST NOT introduce
   209	  commits whose history does not descend from the proposal's
   210	  declared parents.
   211	- The implementer MAY produce multiple commits on the worker
   212	  branch. The evaluator consumes only the tip (`commit_sha`); the
   213	  integrator's squash rule is defined in
   214	  [`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md).
   215	
   216	### 3.4 Submission
   217	
   218	The implementer submits with:
   219	
   220	- `trial_id` — the trial it created.
   221	- `status` — one of:
   222	  - `"success"` — the `work/*` branch tip is `commit_sha` and is
   223	    ready to be evaluated.
   224	  - `"error"` — the implementer could not realize the proposal.
   225	    The trial MUST be persisted with `status == "error"`. No
   226	    evaluate task is dispatched against an errored trial.
   227	- `commit_sha` — required when `status == "success"`; MUST equal
   228	  the tip of the worker branch.
   229	
   230	A resubmission of the same `implement` task MUST be idempotent: a
   231	duplicate submit presenting the same `trial_id` and `commit_sha`
   232	MUST be accepted without side effect. A duplicate submit that
   233	disagrees with the already-recorded result MUST be rejected
   234	([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.2).
   235	
   236	## 4. Evaluator
   237	
   238	The evaluator measures a completed trial against the experiment's
   239	metrics schema.
   240	
   241	### 4.1 Inputs
   242	
   243	An evaluator receives:
   244	
   245	- The task object (`kind == "evaluate"`,
   246	  `payload.trial_id == T`).
   247	- Read access to the trial `T`. The orchestrator MUST ensure
   248	  `T.status == "starting"` and `T.commit_sha` is set before
   249	  dispatch.
   250	- Read access to the git repository at `T.commit_sha` on the
   251	  worker branch (`T.branch`). Repository location and access are
   252	  a role-binding concern, as for the implementer (§3.1).
   253	- Read access to the experiment's `metrics_schema` and
   254	  `objective`.
   255	
   256	### 4.2 Outputs
   257	
   258	The evaluator MUST:
   259	
   260	1. Produce a `metrics` object whose keys are a subset of the
   261	   declared `metrics_schema` keys and whose values satisfy the
   262	   per-metric type rules
   263	   ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §1.3, §7.2).
   264	2. Optionally upload supporting artifacts (logs, captured outputs,
   265	   diagnostic files).
   266	
   267	The evaluator MUST NOT modify the worker branch or any
   268	protocol-owned mutable state other than the trial fields the
   269	submission writes (§4.4) and the task it holds a claim on. In
   270	particular, the evaluator MUST NOT write to the trial's
   271	`completed_at`, `metrics`, `artifacts_uri`, `description`, or
   272	`status` directly; those writes are performed by the orchestrator
   273	when the submitted task reaches its terminal state (§4.4,
   274	[`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.3).
   275	
   276	### 4.3 Non-interference
   277	
   278	The evaluator reads the repository at the trial's worker-branch tip
   279	but MUST NOT push, rewrite, or delete that branch, and MUST NOT
   280	write to the canonical trial lineage. Any side effects of evaluation

1	# Task Protocol
     2	
     3	This chapter specifies the behavioral contract for tasks: the state
     4	machine they advance through, the claim tokens that grant exclusive
     5	execution, the idempotency rules for submission, and the
     6	reclamation policy that bounds worker liveness. It pins *what*
     7	observable behavior a conforming task store and a conforming
     8	orchestrator must exhibit; it does not pin *how* either is
     9	implemented.
    10	
    11	The task object's shape is defined in
    12	[`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3; its JSON Schema is
    13	[`schemas/task.schema.json`](schemas/task.schema.json). The role
    14	contracts that drive the transitions defined here are in
    15	[`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md).
    16	
    17	## 1. State machine
    18	
    19	### 1.1 States
    20	
    21	A task's `state` field takes one of five values. Each has a
    22	well-defined meaning in the state machine:
    23	
    24	| State | Meaning |
    25	|---|---|
    26	| `pending` | The task has been created and is eligible to be claimed. No worker currently holds it. |
    27	| `claimed` | A worker holds an active claim on the task and is expected to execute it. The task carries a `claim` object. |
    28	| `submitted` | The claiming worker has presented a result. The task awaits processing by the orchestrator. |
    29	| `completed` | The orchestrator has accepted the result. The task is terminal. |
    30	| `failed` | The orchestrator has rejected the result, the worker declared failure, or a policy limit (e.g. retry budget) has been exceeded. The task is terminal. |
    31	
    32	`completed` and `failed` are **terminal**: a conforming task store
    33	MUST NOT transition a task out of either state.
    34	
    35	### 1.2 Transitions
    36	
    37	The permitted transitions are exactly:
    38	
    39	```text
    40	          claim                 submit                accept
    41	pending ─────────▶ claimed ─────────────▶ submitted ─────────▶ completed
    42	   ▲                 │                       │
    43	   │ reclaim         │ reclaim               │ reject
    44	   └─────────────────┴───────────────────────┤           ──▶ failed
    45	                                             │
    46	```
    47	
    48	- **claim** — `pending → claimed`. Atomic. Issues a fresh claim
    49	  token. §3.
    50	- **submit** — `claimed → submitted`. Requires presenting the
    51	  current claim token. Idempotent under identical payload.
    52	  Performed for every completed attempt, whether the worker
    53	  declares success or failure. §4.
    54	- **reclaim** — `claimed → pending` or `submitted → pending`.
    55	  Invalidates the prior claim token. §5.
    56	- **accept** — `submitted → completed`. Orchestrator-initiated.
    57	  §4.3.
    58	- **reject** — `submitted → failed`. Orchestrator-initiated,
    59	  including when the worker's own submission declared failure.
    60	  §4.3.
    61	
    62	Any transition not listed above MUST be rejected by the task store.
    63	A conforming task store MUST treat concurrent transition attempts
    64	as serialized: whichever attempt wins the race succeeds, and the
    65	others MUST receive a well-defined rejection that lets the caller
    66	distinguish "invalid" from "raced".
    67	
    68	### 1.3 Event emission
    69	
    70	Every state transition MUST be accompanied by a corresponding event
    71	appended to the event log, atomically with the state change. The
    72	event types (e.g. `task.claimed`, `task.submitted`, `task.failed`)
    73	and the transactional invariant are specified in
    74	[`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md). This chapter does
    75	not enumerate the event shapes; it requires only that they exist
    76	and that no state change is observable without its event.
    77	
    78	## 2. Creating a task
    79	
    80	A task enters the system in the `pending` state. A conforming
    81	orchestrator MUST:
    82	
    83	- Populate `task_id`, `kind`, `state = "pending"`, `payload`,
    84	  `created_at`, `updated_at` ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md)
    85	  §3.1).
    86	- Ensure the payload satisfies the dispatch preconditions for the
    87	  task's `kind`:
    88	  - `plan` — `payload.experiment_id` names an experiment the
    89	    planner is authorized to work on.
    90	  - `implement` — `payload.proposal_id` names a proposal with
    91	    `state == "ready"` at creation time. Creating the implement
    92	    task and transitioning the proposal from `"ready"` to
    93	    `"dispatched"` MUST be atomic; a proposal's `"dispatched"`
    94	    state signals that exactly one implement task has been
    95	    created for it.
    96	  - `evaluate` — `payload.trial_id` names a trial with
    97	    `status == "starting"` and `commit_sha` set at creation time.
    98	- Emit the corresponding `task.created` event (
    99	  [`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md)) atomically with
   100	  the task insert.
   101	
   102	A worker MAY rely on these preconditions ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md)
   103	§1.1). A task created without them is a protocol violation by the
   104	orchestrator.
   105	
   106	## 3. Claim
   107	
   108	### 3.1 Atomicity
   109	
   110	The `pending → claimed` transition MUST be atomic with respect to
   111	competing claim attempts: at most one worker MAY succeed for any
   112	given task. A conforming task store MUST provide this guarantee as
   113	part of its durability contract
   114	([`08-storage.md`](08-storage.md)).
   115	
   116	### 3.2 Claim token
   117	
   118	A successful claim issues a `claim` object
   119	([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3.4) containing:
   120	
   121	- `token` — an opaque value that MUST be:
   122	  - **unique** — no two distinct claims (on the same or different
   123	    tasks) share the same token within an experiment;
   124	  - **unforgeable** — a worker that did not receive the token MUST
   125	    NOT be able to construct a byte-equal value with practical
   126	    effort. Implementations commonly use a cryptographically
   127	    random value; the protocol does not mandate the mechanism.
   128	- `worker_id` — an identifier the worker supplies at claim time.
   129	  The task store MAY record it for audit but MUST NOT use it as a
   130	  substitute for the token when authorizing subsequent operations.
   131	- `claimed_at` — the time the claim was issued.
   132	- `expires_at` — OPTIONAL. If present, the task store MAY reclaim
   133	  the task when the current time exceeds this value (§5).
   134	
   135	### 3.3 Authorization by token
   136	
   137	Every subsequent operation on a claimed task (progress update,
   138	submit, release) MUST present the current claim token. The task
   139	store MUST reject any such operation whose token does not match the
   140	token currently recorded on the task. This is the sole mechanism
   141	the protocol relies on to guarantee single-writer access to a
   142	claimed task.
   143	
   144	### 3.4 No re-claim while claimed
   145	
   146	A conforming task store MUST reject a claim attempt against a task
   147	whose `state` is not `pending`. In particular, a claimed task
   148	cannot be "re-claimed" by a different worker; the prior claim must
   149	first be released (by submission) or invalidated (by reclamation).
   150	
   151	## 4. Submit
   152	
   153	### 4.1 Submission operation
   154	
   155	A submit operation requires:
   156	
   157	- The `task_id`.
   158	- The current claim `token`.
   159	- A result payload whose shape depends on `kind`
   160	  ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §2.4, §3.4, §4.4).
   161	
   162	A successful submit transitions `claimed → submitted` atomically
   163	with persisting the result payload. The terminal transition to
   164	`completed` or `failed` is the orchestrator's subsequent step
   165	(§4.3), even when the submission itself declared failure.
   166	
   167	### 4.2 Idempotency
   168	
   169	A resubmission is a second submit carrying the token recorded on
   170	the task's `claim` object (which is retained through `submitted`
   171	per [`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3.4). A resubmit against
   172	a task whose claim has been cleared — because the task was
   173	reclaimed or reached a terminal state — MUST be rejected
   174	regardless of the presented token (§4.4, §5.2).
   175	
   176	When the token matches, the task store MUST handle the
   177	resubmission as follows:
   178	
   179	- If the resubmission's result payload is **content-equivalent** to
   180	  the already-recorded payload, the task store MUST accept it and
   181	  MUST NOT change the task's state or recorded result. "Content
   182	  equivalence" means the normative fields identified per role
   183	  agree:
   184	  - `plan` — the set of `proposal_ids` (compared as sets; order
   185	    is not significant per [`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §2.4) and
   186	    `status`.
   187	  - `implement` — `trial_id`, `status`, and `commit_sha`
   188	    (when present).
   189	  - `evaluate` — `trial_id`, `status`, and `metrics` (compared as
   190	    JSON values; key order does not matter).
   191	- If the resubmission's result payload is **not** content-equivalent,
   192	  the task store MUST reject it. The first submission's result is
   193	  the committed result.
   194	
   195	This rule exists so that a worker may safely retry a submit after a
   196	network or process failure without risk of advancing state twice or
   197	corrupting the recorded result.
   198	
   199	### 4.3 From `submitted` to terminal
   200	
   201	A submitted task is processed by the orchestrator and transitions
   202	to a terminal state. The orchestrator MUST:
   203	
   204	- Transition `submitted → completed` when the submission's
   205	  `status` is `"success"` and the result satisfies the role's
   206	  success contract (e.g. an implementer submission carries a
   207	  reachable `commit_sha`).
   208	- Transition `submitted → failed` when either:
   209	  - The result violates the role's success contract (malformed
   210	    payload, missing required field, failed post-validation).
   211	  - The worker itself declared failure:
   212	    - `plan` task, worker `status == "error"` → `failed`.
   213	    - `implement` task, worker `status == "error"` → `failed`.
   214	    - `evaluate` task, worker `status == "error"` → `failed`
   215	      AND the referenced trial's `status` MUST be updated to
   216	      `"error"`.
   217	    - `evaluate` task, worker `status == "eval_error"` →
   218	      `failed`, but the referenced trial's `status` MUST remain
   219	      `"starting"` (the trial itself was not shown to be bad;
   220	      only the evaluator-side attempt failed). A fresh
   221	      `evaluate` task MAY be created for the same trial; the
   222	      protocol does not mandate automatic retry. If the
   223	      orchestrator's policy exhausts retries for this trial
   224	      (or the operator abandons it), the orchestrator MUST
   225	      transition the trial's `status` from `"starting"` to
   226	      `"eval_error"`, at which point the trial status is
   227	      terminal.
   228	
   229	Every terminal transition produces exactly one state-change event
   230	in the log ([`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md)) and
   231	MUST clear the task's `claim` object
   232	([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3.4). The orchestrator MAY
   233	implement the `claimed → submitted → completed` (or `failed`)
   234	sequence as two distinct persisted states or as a single store
   235	transaction whose event stream includes both transitions, but
   236	subscribers MUST observe both events in order.
   237	
   238	### 4.4 Post-terminal writes prohibited
   239	
   240	Once a task is `completed` or `failed`, no further writes to its
   241	task fields are permitted beyond audit-only metadata that a task
   242	store chooses to expose outside the normative schema
   243	([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §3.1). In particular, a
   244	resubmission against a terminal task MUST be rejected regardless of
   245	content equivalence; the claim token that produced the terminal
   246	transition is no longer valid.
   247	
   248	## 5. Reclamation
   249	
   250	### 5.1 When reclamation is permitted
   251	
   252	A conforming task store MAY reclaim a task — move it from
   253	`claimed` back to `pending` — in any of the following cases:
   254	
   255	- The `claim.expires_at` timestamp has passed.
   256	- An operator invokes an explicit reclaim action on the task.
   257	- A task-store-defined health policy determines the claiming worker
   258	  is unreachable.
   259	
   260	A task in `submitted` state MAY be reclaimed **only** by an
   261	explicit operator action; automatic reclaim (expires_at, health
   262	policy) MUST NOT apply to `submitted` tasks. After submit the
   263	worker's reachability is no longer a relevant liveness signal; the
   264	orchestrator is expected to advance the task to a terminal state
   265	(§4.3).
   266	
   267	A task store MUST NOT reclaim a task in a terminal state.
   268	
   269	### 5.2 Effect of reclamation
   270	
   271	Reclamation:
   272	
   273	1. Invalidates the prior claim token. Subsequent operations
   274	   presenting it MUST be rejected.
   275	2. Clears the `claim` object from the task.
   276	3. Sets the task's `state` back to `pending`.
   277	4. Is accompanied by a `task.reclaimed` event in the event log
   278	   ([`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md)), atomically
   279	   with the state change.
   280	
   281	Any partial result a worker had assembled before reclamation is
   282	no longer observable through the task protocol. If the worker
   283	created store-resident objects (drafting proposals, starting
   284	trials), those objects persist per their own lifecycle and are
   285	subject to role-level cleanup rules; reclamation itself does not
   286	delete them.
   287	
   288	### 5.3 Worker detection of reclamation
   289	
   290	A worker that wishes to detect its own reclamation MAY observe its
   291	task's state or subscribe to events. If a worker discovers its
   292	token has been invalidated, it MUST discontinue work on the task
   293	and MUST NOT submit ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §1 step 5).
   294	
   295	### 5.4 Reclamation effect on role outputs
   296	
   297	When a reclaimed task had created role-owned objects during its
   298	prior execution, the orchestrator MUST reconcile those objects as
   299	follows:
   300	
   301	- **Implement reclamation.** If the prior execution created a
   302	  trial with `status == "starting"`, the orchestrator MUST
   303	  transition that trial's `status` to `"error"` atomically with
   304	  the reclaim event. A subsequent re-claim of the implement task
   305	  produces a **new** trial; starting trials are never shared
   306	  across implementer attempts.
   307	- **Plan reclamation.** If the prior execution persisted
   308	  proposals in `"drafting"` state, those proposals remain in
   309	  `"drafting"` and are not dispatched. Implementations MAY expose
   310	  them to operators for inspection or removal; the task protocol
   311	  does not mandate automatic cleanup.
   312	- **Evaluate reclamation.** The trial the task referenced is
   313	  unchanged by reclamation (it was produced by the implementer,
   314	  not the evaluator).
   315	
   316	## 6. Ordering and concurrency
   317	
   318	### 6.1 Per-task serialization
   319	
   320	All transitions on a single task MUST be serialized: the observable

320	All transitions on a single task MUST be serialized: the observable
   321	history of a task is a total order. A conforming task store MUST
   322	NOT expose a state to readers that has not yet been accompanied by
   323	its event.
   324	
   325	### 6.2 Cross-task concurrency
   326	
   327	Distinct tasks MAY progress concurrently. The protocol imposes no
   328	global ordering on transitions across tasks beyond the causal order
   329	preserved by the event log
   330	([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §4.3,
   331	[`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md)).
   332	
   333	### 6.3 Observability
   334	
   335	A subscriber that reads the event log MUST be able to reconstruct
   336	every task's state-machine history exactly. Implementations MAY
   337	provide faster paths (direct task reads, push notifications) but
   338	MUST NOT let those paths expose states that the event log does not
   339	record.
   340	
   341	## 7. Proposal and trial lifecycle interactions
   342	
   343	The task state machine is not the only lifecycle in the system.
   344	Proposals ([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §5.1) and trials
   345	([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §7) have their own state
   346	fields. The task protocol interacts with these lifecycles at the
   347	following points:
   348	
   349	- **Plan submit.** A successful plan-task submission MAY reference
   350	  proposals that have been transitioned to `state == "ready"` by
   351	  the planner ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §2.2). The task
   352	  protocol does not itself transition proposal state; it treats
   353	  the proposal as a value produced by the planner.
   354	- **Implement submit.** A successful implement-task submission
   355	  requires a trial created and advanced by the implementer
   356	  ([`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §3.2). On any terminal transition
   357	  of the implement task (whether `submitted → completed` or
   358	  `submitted → failed`), the orchestrator MUST transition the
   359	  referenced proposal's `state` from `"dispatched"` to
   360	  `"completed"`, atomically with the task's terminal event. The
   361	  proposal's `"completed"` state therefore means *"the
   362	  implementer's attempt on this proposal has finished"*, not
   363	  *"the attempt succeeded"*; the trial's `status` field records
   364	  the outcome. A proposal in `"dispatched"` always names a
   365	  non-terminal implement task; no proposal remains in
   366	  `"dispatched"` after its implement task has reached a terminal
   367	  state.
   368	- **Evaluate submit.** A successful evaluate-task submission
   369	  populates the trial's `metrics`, `completed_at`, and (per
   370	  worker-declared status) the trial's `status`. The integrator's
   371	  subsequent promotion of the trial into the canonical lineage is
   372	  out of this chapter's scope
   373	  ([`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md)).
   374	
   375	## 8. Implementation latitude
   376	
   377	The protocol leaves to implementations:
   378	
   379	- The mechanism by which a worker discovers claimable tasks
   380	  (polling, subscription, dispatch).
   381	- The representation of claim tokens, worker IDs, and task IDs.
   382	- The timeout and reclamation policy, including whether to set
   383	  `expires_at` by default.
   384	- Whether retries are automatic (new task on reclamation) or
   385	  operator-driven.
   386	- Whether `submitted → completed` is fully synchronous with submit
   387	  or a subsequent orchestrator step, as long as the observable
   388	  state machine above is preserved.
   389	
   390	What the protocol does **not** leave to implementations:
   391	
   392	- The set of states and the permitted transitions among them (§1).
   393	- The atomicity of claim (§3.1) and the token-authorization rule
   394	  (§3.3).
   395	- The idempotency rule for resubmission (§4.2).
   396	- The requirement that every state change be accompanied by an
   397	  atomic event (§1.3) and that the event log is the normative
   398	  observability channel (§6.3).

1	"""Shared fixtures: schema registry, per-schema validators, model validators.
     2	
     3	The parity tests need to run each case twice — once against the Pydantic
     4	model and once against the hand-authored JSON Schema. This conftest wires
     5	up both paths over the same case corpus so they stay in sync.
     6	
     7	A custom ``FormatChecker`` enables ``uri`` and ``date-time`` format
     8	validation on the schema side; jsonschema's default does not enforce
     9	``format`` keywords unless a checker is supplied. The model side enforces
    10	the same semantics in :mod:`eden_contracts._common`.
    11	"""
    12	
    13	from __future__ import annotations
    14	
    15	import json
    16	from collections.abc import Callable
    17	from datetime import datetime
    18	from pathlib import Path
    19	from typing import Any
    20	
    21	from eden_contracts import (
    22	    REGISTERED_EVENT_TYPES,
    23	    Event,
    24	    ExperimentConfig,
    25	    MetricsSchema,
    26	    Proposal,
    27	    RegisteredEventAdapter,
    28	    TaskAdapter,
    29	    Trial,
    30	)
    31	from jsonschema import Draft202012Validator, FormatChecker
    32	from referencing import Registry, Resource
    33	from referencing.jsonschema import DRAFT202012
    34	from rfc3986_validator import validate_rfc3986
    35	
    36	SCHEMAS_DIR: Path = Path(__file__).resolve().parents[4] / "spec" / "v0" / "schemas"
    37	"""Resolves to ``<repo>/spec/v0/schemas``."""
    38	
    39	BASE_URI: str = "https://eden.local/schemas/"
    40	"""Synthetic base URI so relative ``$ref``s between schema files resolve."""
    41	
    42	MODEL_NAMES: tuple[str, ...] = (
    43	    "experiment-config",
    44	    "task",
    45	    "event",
    46	    "proposal",
    47	    "trial",
    48	    "metrics-schema",
    49	)
    50	
    51	
    52	FORMAT_CHECKER = FormatChecker()
    53	
    54	EXPLICIT_FORMATS: set[str] = set()
    55	"""Formats with a custom handler registered below.
    56	
    57	``jsonschema.FormatChecker()`` pre-registers permissive defaults for
    58	many well-known formats (``uri``, ``email``, ``uuid``, …), so a naive
    59	"is this format in the checker?" test green-lights any format at all.
    60	We want parity to be explicit — every format the schemas use has a
    61	handler we wrote, with matching enforcement on the model side — so
    62	we track our deliberate registrations separately from the built-ins.
    63	"""
    64	
    65	
    66	def _register_format(name: str) -> Any:
    67	    def decorator(func: Any) -> Any:
    68	        EXPLICIT_FORMATS.add(name)
    69	        FORMAT_CHECKER.checks(name, raises=ValueError)(func)
    70	        return func
    71	
    72	    return decorator
    73	
    74	
    75	@_register_format("uri")
    76	def _check_uri(instance: Any) -> bool:
    77	    if not isinstance(instance, str):
    78	        return True
    79	    if not validate_rfc3986(instance, rule="URI"):
    80	        raise ValueError(f"not a valid RFC 3986 URI: {instance!r}")
    81	    return True
    82	
    83	
    84	@_register_format("date-time")
    85	def _check_datetime(instance: Any) -> bool:
    86	    if not isinstance(instance, str):
    87	        return True
    88	    datetime.fromisoformat(instance)
    89	    return True
    90	
    91	
    92	def _build_registry() -> Registry:
    93	    registry: Registry = Registry()
    94	    for path in SCHEMAS_DIR.glob("*.schema.json"):
    95	        schema = json.loads(path.read_text())
    96	        resource = Resource.from_contents(schema, default_specification=DRAFT202012)
    97	        registry = registry.with_resource(uri=f"{BASE_URI}{path.name}", resource=resource)
    98	    return registry
    99	
   100	
   101	_REGISTRY: Registry = _build_registry()
   102	
   103	
   104	def schema_validator(name: str) -> Draft202012Validator:
   105	    """Return a validator for ``<name>.schema.json`` with refs + formats enforced."""
   106	    return Draft202012Validator(
   107	        schema={"$ref": f"{BASE_URI}{name}.schema.json"},
   108	        registry=_REGISTRY,
   109	        format_checker=FORMAT_CHECKER,
   110	    )
   111	
   112	
   113	def _validate_event(data: Any) -> object:
   114	    """Envelope-first, then per-type payload for registered types.
   115	
   116	    Mirrors the schema's ``allOf`` dispatch: every event MUST satisfy the
   117	    envelope; events whose ``type`` is registered (spec §3.1–§3.3) MUST
   118	    additionally satisfy the per-type payload shape.
   119	    """
   120	    envelope = Event.model_validate(data)
   121	    if envelope.type in REGISTERED_EVENT_TYPES:
   122	        RegisteredEventAdapter.validate_python(data)
   123	    return envelope
   124	
   125	
   126	_MODEL_VALIDATORS: dict[str, Callable[[Any], object]] = {
   127	    "experiment-config": ExperimentConfig.model_validate,
   128	    "task": TaskAdapter.validate_python,
   129	    "event": _validate_event,
   130	    "proposal": Proposal.model_validate,
   131	    "trial": Trial.model_validate,
   132	    "metrics-schema": MetricsSchema.model_validate,
   133	}
   134	
   135	
   136	def model_validate(name: str, data: Any) -> object:
   137	    """Validate ``data`` with the Pydantic model bound to schema ``name``."""
   138	    return _MODEL_VALIDATORS[name](data)

1	"""Round-trip parity: every accept fixture dumps to schema-valid JSON.
     2	
     3	``model_validate(data) → model_dump(mode="json", exclude_none=True)`` MUST
     4	produce output that is accepted by the JSON Schema. ``exclude_none=True``
     5	is the idiomatic dump mode for wire-format emission — JSON Schema treats
     6	optional fields as absent-or-present, not nullable, so a naive dump that
     7	includes ``null`` keys would fail schema validation.
     8	"""
     9	
    10	from __future__ import annotations
    11	
    12	from typing import Any
    13	
    14	from eden_contracts import (
    15	    REGISTERED_EVENT_TYPES,
    16	    Event,
    17	    ExperimentConfig,
    18	    MetricsSchema,
    19	    Proposal,
    20	    RegisteredEventAdapter,
    21	    TaskAdapter,
    22	    Trial,
    23	)
    24	
    25	from .cases import ALL_CASES
    26	from .conftest import schema_validator
    27	
    28	
    29	def _dump_event(data: Any) -> Any:
    30	    """Dump through the registered-type model when ``type`` is registered.
    31	
    32	    An envelope-only dump would discard per-type payload validation, which
    33	    would mask round-trip drift for registered events.
    34	    """
    35	    envelope = Event.model_validate(data)
    36	    if envelope.type in REGISTERED_EVENT_TYPES:
    37	        typed = RegisteredEventAdapter.validate_python(data)
    38	        return RegisteredEventAdapter.dump_python(typed, mode="json", exclude_none=True)
    39	    return envelope.model_dump(mode="json", exclude_none=True)
    40	
    41	
    42	_MODEL_DUMPERS = {
    43	    "experiment-config": lambda d: ExperimentConfig.model_validate(d).model_dump(
    44	        mode="json", exclude_none=True
    45	    ),
    46	    "task": lambda d: TaskAdapter.dump_python(
    47	        TaskAdapter.validate_python(d), mode="json", exclude_none=True
    48	    ),
    49	    "event": _dump_event,
    50	    "proposal": lambda d: Proposal.model_validate(d).model_dump(mode="json", exclude_none=True),
    51	    "trial": lambda d: Trial.model_validate(d).model_dump(mode="json", exclude_none=True),
    52	    "metrics-schema": lambda d: MetricsSchema.model_validate(d).model_dump(
    53	        mode="json", exclude_none=True
    54	    ),
    55	}
    56	
    57	
    58	def test_accept_fixtures_roundtrip_schema_valid() -> None:
    59	    """Every accept case dumps back to JSON that the schema validates."""
    60	    for schema_name, cases in ALL_CASES.items():
    61	        validator = schema_validator(schema_name)
    62	        dump = _MODEL_DUMPERS[schema_name]
    63	        for case in cases:
    64	            if not case.should_pass:
    65	                continue
    66	            dumped = dump(case.data)
    67	            validator.validate(dumped)

256	## 6. Metrics schema
   257	
   258	Schema: [`schemas/metrics-schema.schema.json`](schemas/metrics-schema.schema.json).
   259	
   260	A metrics schema is a mapping from metric name to storage type. It
   261	appears as the `metrics_schema` field of an experiment config and is
   262	used to validate every metrics payload the evaluator produces.
   263	
   264	### 6.1 Shape
   265	
   266	The metrics schema is a JSON object whose keys are metric names and
   267	whose values are type strings.
   268	
   269	- A metric name MUST match `^[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*$`.
   270	- A type value MUST be one of `"integer"`, `"real"`, `"text"`.
   271	- A metrics schema MUST have at least one entry.
   272	
   273	### 6.2 Reserved names
   274	
   275	The names `trial_id`, `commit_sha`, `parent_commits`, `branch`,
   276	`status`, `artifacts_uri`, `description`, `timestamp`, `started_at`,
   277	`completed_at` are reserved by the protocol for use on the trial
   278	object and MUST NOT appear in a metrics schema.
   279	
   280	## 7. Trial
   281	
   282	Schema: [`schemas/trial.schema.json`](schemas/trial.schema.json).
   283	
   284	A trial is one completed attempt.
   285	
   286	### 7.1 Fields
   287	
   288	| Field | Required | Type | Description |
   289	|---|---|---|---|
   290	| `trial_id` | yes | string | Unique identifier. |
   291	| `experiment_id` | yes | string | The experiment this trial belongs to. |
   292	| `proposal_id` | yes | string | The proposal that produced this trial. |
   293	| `status` | yes | string | One of `"starting"`, `"success"`, `"error"`, `"eval_error"`. |
   294	| `parent_commits` | yes | array of SHA | Inherited from the proposal. |
   295	| `branch` | no | string | Worker branch under `work/*`. Present once the implementer starts. |
   296	| `commit_sha` | no | string | Worker-branch tip SHA. Present once the implementer completes. |
   297	| `trial_commit_sha` | no | string | Canonical-lineage SHA under `trial/*`. Present once the integrator has promoted the trial. |
   298	| `artifacts_uri` | no | string (URI) | Where the trial's artifacts live. |
   299	| `description` | no | string | Human-readable summary. |
   300	| `metrics` | no | object | Metrics payload; shape dictated by the experiment's metrics schema. |
   301	| `started_at` | yes | timestamp | When the implementer began. |
   302	| `completed_at` | no | timestamp | Set when the trial reaches a terminal status. Written exactly once by the orchestrator, atomically with the transition from `"starting"` to `"success"`, `"error"`, or `"eval_error"` (see [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.3 and [`03-roles.md`](03-roles.md) §4.4). |
   303	
   304	### 7.2 Metrics payload
   305	
   306	If present, `metrics` MUST be an object whose keys are a subset of
   307	the declared metrics-schema keys and whose values match the declared
   308	types (§1.3) or are `null`. Because the metrics-schema is
   309	per-experiment, [`schemas/trial.schema.json`](schemas/trial.schema.json)
   310	cannot express this constraint generically and leaves `metrics` as
   311	an open object; the per-metric type check is a runtime responsibility
   312	of the orchestrator. A conforming orchestrator MUST reject a metrics
   313	payload that violates the experiment's metrics-schema, and MUST NOT
   314	record the trial as `"success"` in that case.
   315	
   316	### 7.3 Status transitions
   317	
   318	Trial status transitions are behavioral and are defined alongside
   319	the task and event protocols in
   320	[`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md). The data model
   321	constrains only that the status be one of the listed strings.
   322	
   323	## 8. Invariants across entities
   324	
   325	This section collects structural invariants that span multiple
   326	entities. Behavioral invariants (claim semantics, transactional
   327	event writes) live in the corresponding behavior chapters.
   328	
   329	1. **Identifier scope.** `task_id`, `proposal_id`, `trial_id`, and
   330	   `event_id` MUST each be unique within a single experiment.
   331	   Uniqueness across experiments is not required by the protocol
   332	   but is RECOMMENDED when an implementation shares a store across
   333	   experiments.
   334	2. **Reference integrity.** Every `proposal_id` referenced by a
   335	   task payload or trial MUST name a proposal persisted in the
   336	   store before the reference is written. Every `trial_id`
   337	   referenced by an `evaluate` task MUST exist with
   338	   `status == "starting"` at dispatch time.
   339	3. **Metrics/schema conformance.** Every metrics payload persisted
   340	   on a trial MUST validate against the experiment's
   341	   `metrics_schema` (§6).
   342	4. **Canonical-lineage single-writer.** Only the integrator MAY
   343	   write to `trial_commit_sha` on a trial. Workers MUST NOT set or
   344	   modify this field directly.
   345	5. **Claim uniqueness.** A task MAY have at most one active
   346	   `claim` object at any time.

280	write to the canonical trial lineage. Any side effects of evaluation
   281	(test caches, build outputs, captured logs) MUST NOT be observable
   282	through any protocol-owned store, ref, or artifact location except
   283	via the evaluator's declared outputs (§4.2).
   284	
   285	### 4.4 Submission
   286	
   287	The evaluator submits with:
   288	
   289	- `trial_id` — the trial it evaluated.
   290	- `status` — one of:
   291	  - `"success"` — the trial ran and produced metrics.
   292	  - `"error"` — the trial could not be evaluated for reasons
   293	    attributable to the trial's own code (build failure, test
   294	    failure, etc.). The evaluator MAY still include partial
   295	    metrics.
   296	  - `"eval_error"` — the evaluator itself failed for reasons
   297	    unrelated to the trial's code (infrastructure fault,
   298	    evaluator bug). While a fresh evaluate task MAY still be
   299	    created for this trial, the trial's status MUST remain
   300	    `"starting"`. If the orchestrator's retry policy is
   301	    exhausted (or the operator abandons evaluation), the
   302	    orchestrator MUST transition the trial's status to
   303	    `"eval_error"`, making that status terminal for the trial
   304	    ([`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.3).
   305	- `metrics` — the metrics object described in §4.2. MAY be absent
   306	  when `status == "eval_error"`.
   307	- `artifacts_uri` — OPTIONAL. A URI the evaluator uploaded
   308	  supporting artifacts to.
   309	
   310	On a `submitted → completed` or `submitted → failed` transition
   311	(per [`04-task-protocol.md`](04-task-protocol.md) §4.3), the
   312	orchestrator MUST write the following trial fields atomically with
   313	the event:
   314	
   315	- `status` — the trial status implied by the submission: `"success"`
   316	  when the submission's `status == "success"`; `"error"` when the
   317	  submission's `status == "error"`; unchanged from `"starting"`
   318	  when the submission's `status == "eval_error"` (see §4.4 above
   319	  for the terminal-retry case).
   320	- `metrics` — set to the submission's `metrics` when
   321	  `status ∈ {"success", "error"}`. When `status == "eval_error"`
   322	  the orchestrator MUST NOT write `metrics` on the trial; any
   323	  submission-carried `metrics` is discarded.
   324	- `artifacts_uri` — set to the submission's `artifacts_uri` when
   325	  provided and `status ∈ {"success", "error"}`. When
   326	  `status == "eval_error"` the orchestrator MUST NOT write
   327	  `artifacts_uri` on the trial; any submission-carried
   328	  `artifacts_uri` is discarded. (An evaluator that wishes to
   329	  retain diagnostic artifacts from a failed attempt MAY reference
   330	  them in the `task.failed` event for that evaluate task; that
   331	  channel is defined in
   332	  [`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md).)
   333	- `completed_at` — set to the time of the terminal trial
   334	  transition, i.e. written exactly once, when the trial's status
   335	  leaves `"starting"` (either on a `"success"`/`"error"`
   336	  submission, or on the retry-exhausted `"eval_error"`
   337	  transition). Intermediate `eval_error` submissions MUST NOT
   338	  advance `completed_at`.
   339	
   340	On the retry-exhausted `"eval_error"` terminal transition itself,
   341	the orchestrator MUST NOT graft metrics or artifacts from any
   342	prior `eval_error` submission onto the trial; the trial's
   343	`metrics` and `artifacts_uri` fields remain unset. This keeps the
   344	trial object canonical: a trial either carries the outputs of a
   345	successful or code-level-failed evaluation, or it carries nothing.
   346	
   347	Resubmission is idempotent under the same rules as §3.4: identical
   348	`trial_id` + `status` + `metrics` + `artifacts_uri` MUST be
   349	accepted; inconsistent resubmission MUST be rejected.
   350	
   351	## 5. Integrator
   352	
   353	The integrator promotes successfully evaluated trials into the
   354	canonical trial lineage.
   355	
   356	The integrator's full contract — the squash rule, the eval-manifest
   357	shape, conflict-resolution policy — is deferred to
   358	[`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md). This section pins only the
   359	boundary rules that every other role must honor.
   360	
   361	### 5.1 Exclusive authority
   362	
   363	The integrator is the **sole writer** of the canonical trial lineage
   364	(`trial/*` branches, `trial_commit_sha` fields on trials). Every
   365	other role MUST treat `trial/*` as read-only and MUST NOT set or
   366	modify a trial's `trial_commit_sha`.
   367	
   368	### 5.2 Inputs
   369	
   370	The integrator observes trials transitioning to `status == "success"`
   371	(as recorded by the evaluator in §4.4). The mechanism of observation
   372	(event subscription, store poll, dispatch) is a binding concern.
   373	
   374	### 5.3 Outputs
   375	
   376	The integrator writes a single commit on a `trial/*` branch for each
   377	promoted trial and records the resulting SHA in the trial's
   378	`trial_commit_sha`. Exact topology invariants are in
   379	[`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md).
   380	
   381	### 5.4 Why integration is separate from evaluation
   382	
   383	Evaluation and integration are separate roles because a successful
   384	evaluation is a necessary but not sufficient condition for promoting
   385	a trial: the integrator applies experiment-level policy
   386	(squash shape, conflict resolution with concurrent trials, manifest
   387	attachment) that is not meaningful to the evaluator. A conforming
   388	implementation MAY collapse evaluator and integrator into the same
   389	process, but MUST still honor both contracts independently.
   390	
   391	## 6. Role binding (deferred)
   392	
   393	How a role is invoked, addressed, or configured is **not specified
   394	in v0**. A conforming deployment MAY:
   395	
   396	- Run each role as a separate long-lived service reached over some
   397	  wire protocol.
   398	- Spawn each role as a short-lived subprocess per task.
   399	- Collapse multiple roles into a single process that consumes its
   400	  own task queue.

260	### 3.5 Non-registered event types
   261	
   262	Implementations MAY emit events whose `type` is not listed in
   263	§3.1–§3.3, provided the type conforms to the `^[a-z][a-z0-9_]*(\.[a-z][a-z0-9_]*)+$`
   264	pattern. Such events carry no protocol-defined semantics and MUST NOT
   265	be required by any conforming subscriber.
   266	
   267	A non-registered event MUST NOT carry a registered `type` with a
   268	non-conforming payload; that is a protocol violation, not an
   269	extension. The registry is closed at v0: a future spec lineage
   270	(`v1`) MAY add or revise registered types.
   271	
   272	## 4. Delivery guarantees
   273	
   274	A conforming event log MUST provide each subscriber the following
   275	guarantees, which subscribers MAY rely on without additional
   276	coordination.
   277	
   278	### 4.1 Total order per experiment
   279	
   280	Within a single experiment, a conforming event log MUST present a
   281	single total order to subscribers. Two subscribers reading the same
   282	experiment's stream MUST observe every pair of events in the same
   283	relative order. The ordering mechanism (monotonic sequence, log
   284	position, vector clock) is a storage concern; the observable
   285	guarantee is not.
   286	
   287	### 4.2 Causal consistency
   288	
   289	Within the per-experiment total order, the sequence MUST respect
   290	causality: if event B describes a state change that depended on a
   291	state recorded by event A, A MUST precede B in the log
   292	([`02-data-model.md`](02-data-model.md) §4.3). In particular, for
   293	every composite commit enumerated in §2.2, the events produced by
   294	that commit MAY appear in any order relative to each other — they
   295	were written atomically and neither causes the other — but they MUST
   296	all precede any event that causally depends on the committed state.
   297	
   298	### 4.3 At-least-once delivery
   299	
   300	A conforming log MUST deliver every appended event to every active
   301	subscriber at least once. A subscriber MAY observe the same
   302	`event_id` more than once under recovery, restart, or reconnect;
   303	subscribers MUST dedupe by `event_id` when exact-once semantics are
   304	required for their projection.
   305	
   306	Implementations MAY offer stronger delivery than at-least-once (e.g.
   307	exactly-once via offset commits), but MUST NOT offer weaker than
   308	at-least-once.
   309	
   310	### 4.4 Replayability
   311	
   312	A conforming log MUST allow a subscriber to replay the full stream
   313	for a given experiment from its first event. This is what lets late
   314	subscribers reconstruct history and lets existing subscribers
   315	recover from projection corruption. The log MAY allow starting from
   316	a caller-supplied position (event ID or offset) as an optimization,
   317	but the complete replay is required.
   318	
   319	### 4.5 Durability
   320	
   321	A successful append MUST be durable through the next crash of the
   322	log implementation. An event the log has acknowledged to the writer
   323	MUST NOT later disappear from any subscriber's stream. Durability
   324	requirements for the log as a store are pinned in
   325	[`08-storage.md`](08-storage.md) §3.
   326	
   327	### 4.6 No cross-experiment ordering
   328	
   329	Events in different experiments have no mandated relative order. A
   330	conforming log MAY expose a global ordering (e.g. a single monotonic
   331	sequence across all experiments) as an implementation convenience,
   332	but a subscriber that relies on cross-experiment order is relying on
   333	something the protocol does not guarantee.
   334	
   335	## 5. Implementation latitude
   336	
   337	The protocol leaves to implementations:
   338	
   339	- The transport and storage of events (append-only table, commit log,
   340	  message broker).
   341	- The subscription mechanism (long-poll, push, WebSocket, `LISTEN`).
   342	- The concrete `event_id` representation, as long as uniqueness (§1.1)
   343	  holds.
   344	- The retention policy — a log MAY compact or archive after a policy
   345	  window, provided replay (§4.4) is preserved for the retained
   346	  window. Consumers that need history beyond the retention policy's
   347	  horizon are responsible for their own durable projections.
   348	- The mechanism of atomicity (§2): transactional database, outbox
   349	  pattern, two-phase commit across task store and log, or a unified
   350	  store where the log *is* the task store's write-ahead record.
   351	
   352	What the protocol does **not** leave to implementations:
   353	
   354	- The envelope fields (§1).
   355	- The transactional invariant (§2).
   356	- The registered event types and their payloads (§3.1–§3.3).
   357	- The delivery guarantees in §4.

   261	`sha256` field is required so a later consumer can verify the artifact
   262	has not been altered since integration. The `bytes` field is the
   263	artifact's size in bytes at integration time and is required for the

   265	
   266	If the trial's evaluator uploaded no artifacts, `artifacts` MUST be

   268	
   269	### 4.4 `evaluator` shape
   270	
   271	```json
   272	{
   273	  "name": "<implementation-chosen identifier for the evaluator>",
   274	  "version": "<version string the evaluator presented at submission>"
   275	}
   276	```
   277	
   278	Both fields are required. `name` and `version` together identify
   279	*which* evaluator measured the trial; they are recorded so a consumer
   280	revisiting the trial months later can recover whether a given metric
   281	is comparable across trials or not (re-evaluator replacement is a
   282	lineage concern).
   283	
   284	### 4.5 Manifest immutability
   285	
   286	Because the eval manifest lives inside the `trial/*` commit's tree,
   287	it inherits the immutability of the `trial/*` branch (§1.2): once
   288	written, it MUST NOT be rewritten. A conforming implementation that
   289	discovers a manifest error after the fact MAY record corrective
   290	state outside git (operator-level event, separate annotation store)
   291	but MUST NOT mutate the `trial/*` commit.
   292	
   293	## 5. Failure modes
   294	
   295	### 5.1 Metrics-schema violation at promotion time
   296	
   297	If the integrator re-validates metrics (per §2) and finds them in
   298	violation of the experiment's `metrics_schema`, the integrator MUST
   299	NOT produce a `trial/*` commit, MUST NOT write `trial_commit_sha`,
   300	and MUST NOT append `trial.integrated`. It MUST surface the problem
   301	through an implementation-defined operator channel; since the trial's
   302	`status` was written to `success` by the orchestrator at the evaluate
   303	terminal, the promotion-side rejection is a protocol-level drift
   304	between the two that an operator MUST resolve. A conforming
   305	orchestrator makes this case rare by enforcing schema conformance at
   306	the evaluate terminal.
   307	
   308	### 5.2 Partial git write
   309	
   310	If the underlying git ref write appears to succeed but later fails
   311	durability (crashed filesystem, partial replication), the
   312	integrator's atomicity contract (§3.4) is violated. Implementations
   313	MUST ensure ref durability before considering a promotion complete;
   314	conforming storage requirements are pinned in
   315	[`08-storage.md`](08-storage.md) §5 (artifact store) and §3
   316	(durability semantics applied at the git layer by the integrator).
   317	
   318	### 5.3 Repeat promotion
   319	
   320	If the integrator is invoked twice for the same trial (replay of a
   321	`trial.succeeded` event, operator retry), it MUST be idempotent:
   322	
   323	- If the trial already has `trial_commit_sha` set and that SHA
   324	  resolves to a `trial/*` commit whose tree matches the trial's
   325	  `commit_sha` tree and whose eval manifest matches §4, the second
   326	  invocation MUST be a no-op (no new ref, no new event).
   327	- If the trial already has `trial_commit_sha` set but the recorded
   328	  SHA does not resolve or disagrees with the trial, the state is
   329	  corrupt; the integrator MUST NOT silently overwrite. An operator
   330	  channel per §5.1 applies.
   331	
   332	## 6. Implementation latitude
   333	
   334	The protocol leaves to implementations:
   335	
   336	- The git host (local bare repo, Gitea, GitHub, etc.) and the
   337	  transport used to write `trial/*` refs.
   338	- The retention policy for `work/*` branches after promotion.
   339	- The exact author/committer identity stamped on the integrator's
   340	  commits.
   341	- The mechanism of atomicity in §3.4 — outbox, store-level
   342	  transaction, compensating writes.
   343	
   344	What the protocol does **not** leave to implementations:
   345	
   346	- The three-namespace topology and sole-integrator rule (§1).
   347	- The single-commit squash shape and `tree == worker-tip tree` rule
   348	  (§3.2).
   349	- The commit-message format (§3.3).
   350	- The eval-manifest path and required fields (§4).
   351	- The atomic-three invariant (§3.4).

260	### 5.3 Content integrity
   261	
   262	The integrator's eval manifest records a `sha256` and `bytes` field
   263	per artifact ([`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md) §4.3). The
   264	artifact store is not required to compute or verify these at fetch
   265	time — the manifest is the canonical attestation — but it MUST NOT
   266	serve byte content that differs from what was originally uploaded
   267	for a given URI. A store that versions URIs MUST ensure a fetch
   268	against a given URI returns the upload that produced it, not a
   269	subsequent overwrite.
   270	
   271	### 5.4 No protocol-owned mutation after the fact
   272	
   273	Once a protocol-owned object (proposal, trial, eval manifest)
   274	references an artifact URI, a conforming deployment MUST NOT
   275	overwrite the content at that URI. Overwriting would break
   276	reproducibility guarantees that subscribers reading the event log
   277	rely on.
   278	
   279	## 6. Transactional guarantees
   280	
   281	The transactional invariant established in
   282	[`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md) §2 is the central
   283	storage contract:
   284	
   285	> Every state change observable via tasks, proposals, or trials MUST
   286	> be accompanied by a corresponding event, and the event write MUST
   287	> be atomic with the state change it describes.
   288	
   289	A conforming deployment MUST satisfy this invariant for every
   290	operation in §1.1 that mutates protocol-owned state. The mechanism is
   291	implementation-defined. The observable guarantee — that no reader
   292	observes a state change without its event and vice versa — is not.
   293	
   294	### 6.1 Composite commits
   295	
   296	For the multi-entity transitions enumerated in
   297	[`05-event-protocol.md`](05-event-protocol.md) §2.2, every component
   298	of the commit (multiple state changes + multiple event appends) MUST
   299	land together or not at all. A subscriber that observes any one
   300	event of a composite commit MUST be able to observe all of them.
   301	
   302	### 6.2 Failure semantics
   303	
   304	If any component of a composite commit fails, the deployment MUST
   305	roll back all other components. Operator-facing error reporting is
   306	implementation-defined; the observable requirement is that no
   307	partial commit reaches any reader.
   308	
   309	### 6.3 Recovery after partial commit
   310	
   311	If a crash occurs mid-commit, recovery (§3.3) MUST complete or roll
   312	back the transaction such that the composite-commit invariant
   313	(§6.1) holds for every reader after recovery. A conforming
   314	deployment MAY use journal replay, two-phase commit resolution, or
   315	equivalent mechanisms.
   316	
   317	## 7. Implementation latitude
   318	
   319	The protocol leaves to implementations:
   320	
   321	- The storage technology for each store (RDBMS, KV store, embedded
   322	  engine, distributed log, …).
   323	- The wire transport for store access (local library call, HTTP,
   324	  gRPC, direct SQL).
   325	- Whether the three stores are backed by three separate systems,
   326	  one system serving all three, or any combination (e.g. a single
   327	  RDBMS whose outbox table is the event log).
   328	- The retention policies for events, work branches, and artifacts,
   329	  provided the minimum retention floors in §2.4, §5.2, and
   330	  [`06-integrator.md`](06-integrator.md) §1.3 are met.
   331	- The concrete representation of claim tokens, event IDs, and
   332	  URIs.
   333	
   334	What the protocol does **not** leave to implementations:
   335	
   336	- The set of operations each store MUST expose (§1.1, §2.1, §5.1).
   337	- The atomicity contract of each operation (§1, §6).
   338	- The read-after-write and crash-recovery guarantees (§3).
   339	- The metrics-schema enforcement rules (§4).
   340	- The content-immutability rule on artifacts referenced by
   341	  protocol-owned objects (§5.4).

    21	    # components strictly, so it catches non-real dates.

    37	        )

item.text:
**Findings**

1. Bug — [spec/v0/06-integrator.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/06-integrator.md:136) §3.2 conflicts with [spec/v0/06-integrator.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/06-integrator.md:200) §4. The squash rule says the canonical commit `T` MUST satisfy `tree(T) == tree(commit_sha)`, but §4 also requires `T` to contain `.eden/trials/<trial_id>/eval.json`. Those cannot both be true unless the worker branch already contains the manifest, which the implementer/evaluator contracts do not require and effectively forbid. Fix by changing the tree invariant to “worker tip tree plus the eval-manifest path” or by moving the manifest out of the committed tree; then update the repeat-promotion/idempotency check in §5.3 to match.

2. Bug — [spec/v0/06-integrator.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/06-integrator.md:221) §4 requires eval-manifest fields the rest of the protocol never produces. `evaluator.{name,version}` and the per-artifact `{uri, sha256, bytes}` entries have no protocol-defined source: [spec/v0/03-roles.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/03-roles.md:285) only gives evaluator submissions `trial_id`, `status`, `metrics`, and optional `artifacts_uri`, and [spec/v0/08-storage.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/08-storage.md:237) only defines artifact `upload`/`fetch`, not listing or metadata discovery. A conforming integrator cannot build the required manifest from protocol-owned state. Fix by carrying evaluator identity/version and artifact inventory/hash data in the evaluator submission/trial model, or relax the manifest requirements until those fields exist elsewhere in the spec.

3. Bug — [spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:251) §3.4 promises that registered events alone can reconstruct every protocol-owned state change, but the registry payloads do not carry the fields those transitions write. `proposal.drafted`/`proposal.ready` omit proposal contents, `trial.started` omits branch/parents/`started_at`, `trial.succeeded`/`trial.errored` omit metrics/artifacts/`completed_at`, and `task.created` omits task payload/timestamps. [spec/v0/schemas/event.schema.json](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/schemas/event.schema.json:43) and [reference/packages/eden-contracts/src/eden_contracts/event.py](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/reference/packages/eden-contracts/src/eden_contracts/event.py:49) are in lockstep with that minimal registry, so schema/model parity is fine, but the protocol claim is not. Fix by either expanding required event payloads to include the full state delta/snapshot for each protocol-owned write, or narrowing the spec’s reconstruction guarantee to lifecycle transitions only and aligning the cross-chapter observability language.

4. Risk — [spec/v0/08-storage.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/08-storage.md:24) specifies concrete operations only for tasks, events, and artifacts, even though [spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:53) makes the transactional invariant apply equally to proposals and trials and [spec/v0/03-roles.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/03-roles.md:109) requires durable proposal/trial writes. As written, a reimplementation can satisfy the storage chapter literally while leaving proposal/trial persistence semantics under-specified. Fix by defining the proposal/trial repository contract explicitly, or by stating which store owns those writes and that the same durability, read-after-write, and atomicity rules apply.

5. Risk — [spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:310) says replay from an experiment’s first event is required, but [spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/05-event-protocol.md:335) and [spec/v0/08-storage.md](/Users/ericalt/Documents/eden/spec/v0/08-storage.md:134) allow compaction to reject older replays. That ambiguity weakens the replayability floor and leaves room for incompatible implementations. Fix by choosing one contract and stating it consistently: either lifetime full replay while an experiment is retained, or replay only from a declared retention horizon.

**Overall Assessment**

Phase 4 does land the planned 4a+4b+4c artifacts, and I did not find a schema↔model literal drift on the implemented 15-event registry: the enum values and commit-SHA pattern are aligned between `event.schema.json` and `event.py`. I would still request changes before calling the phase coherent. The main blockers are protocol-level: the integrator chapter contains two hard contradictions, and the event/storage chapters currently promise reconstruction and replay guarantees that the defined registry and storage contract do not actually make implementable.
